name: A stability condition for Runge Kutta methods
subsection_title: Order arrows and the Ehle barrier
statement_text:
A Runge–Kutta method is A-stable only if all poles of the
stability function R(z) lie in the right half-plane and no up arrow of the order
web intersects with or is tangential to the imaginary axis.
proof_text:
Proof. The requirement on the poles is obvious. If an up arrow intersects or
is tangential to the imaginary axis then there exists y such that

                              |R(iy) exp(−iy)| > 1.

Because | exp(−iy)| = 1, it follows that |R(iy)| > 1 and the method is not
A-stable.
proof_latex:
\begin{proof}
The requirement on the poles is obvious. If an up arrow intersects or is tangential to the imaginary axis then there exists $y$ such that
\[
|R(iy) \exp(-iy)| > 1.
\]
Because $|\exp(-iy)| = 1$, it follows that $|R(iy)| > 1$ and the method is not A-stable.

We are now in a position to prove the result formerly known as the Ehle conjecture (Ehle, 1973), but which we will also refer to as the `Ehle barrier'.
\end{proof}
context_latex:
\paragraph{Context}
Analysis of A-stability of Runge--Kutta methods uses the order web, where up arrows are associated with poles of the stability function $R(z)$.

variables.name: R(z)
variables.definition: stability function of a Runge–Kutta method

variables.name: up arrow
variables.definition: a graphical element in the order web, representing a direction associated with poles of R(z)

variables.name: order web
variables.definition: a graphical construction used to analyze stability properties

preamble: The context involves analyzing A-stability of Runge–Kutta methods using the order web, where up arrows are associated with poles of the stability function R(z).

dependencies.name: A Stability Criterion for Runge Kutta Methods
